question: An autoscaler is configured to hold CPU at 60%. Traffic doubles instantly and stays there. Roughly how long before the fleet is the right size — given a 60 s metric scrape, a 60 s averaging window, a 60 s decision interval, a 90 s boot and a 60 s warmup?
options: About 60 seconds — the decision interval is the only real delay
About 4-5 minutes, because all of those delays are on the feedback path and add up
About 90 seconds — the boot time dominates and the rest is negligible
Immediately, because target-tracking computes the exact answer in one step
explanation: The delays are in series on the feedback path, so they add: 60 s scrape + 30 s (half the averaging window) + 30 s (half the decision interval) + 90 s boot + 60 s warmup = 270 s = 4.5 minutes. Option D is the tempting one and it is half right: the target-tracking formula IS exact — but only when there is no delay, because then the fleet that produced the metric is the fleet you have. Option A picks the one delay that is easiest to see on a config page and ignores provisioning, which is the largest term.

question: The lesson's simulator ran the identical controller against identical, perfectly flat traffic and varied only the dead time. At 0 s the fleet parked on 24 instances; at 210 s it swung between 6 and 145. What caused the swing?
options: The traffic became bursty once the dead time was increased
The controller's gain was too high and needed a lower setpoint
The loop was acting on measurements older than the time it takes its own actions to show up, so it kept correcting for a state that no longer existed
The instances were failing health checks and being replaced
explanation: The traffic is provably constant across the measured plateau — that is the whole point of the experiment, and it is why the result is about the controller and not the workload. A control loop cannot stabilise a plant whose dead time exceeds its reaction period; the delay turns negative feedback into positive feedback at some frequency and the loop settles into a limit cycle of roughly twice the dead time (measured: 9.6 minutes against a predicted 7.0). Lowering the setpoint (option B) changes where it oscillates around, not whether it oscillates.

question: A service with 8 worker threads normally takes 12 ms per request, of which 6 ms is CPU. Its database degrades and the request now takes 120 ms, still with 6 ms of CPU. The instance saturates at 8 / 0.120 = 67 req/s. What does a CPU-target autoscaler do?
options: Scales out, because 67 req/s is well above the healthy per-instance rate
Holds steady, because CPU utilisation is unchanged at 60%
Scales in, because 67 req/s only uses 40% CPU and the target is 60%
Scales out, but too slowly to matter
explanation: At 67 req/s the instance is completely full — every worker slot is occupied — and it is burning only 67 x 6 ms = 0.4 s of CPU per second, which is 40%. The controller reads 40% against a 60% target and concludes it has too many instances. CPU does not merely lag here; it points the wrong way, because CPU measures work and a thread blocked on a socket is not working. Measured, that run held 15-25 instances while utilisation ran at 2.33x capacity and delivered 45.1% SLO. In-flight requests per instance (Little's Law, L = lambda x W) rises in both regimes, which is why max(CPU, in-flight) is the answer to ship.

question: During a retry storm, arrival rate is 3x real demand and the app tier autoscales from 6 to 20 instances. Each instance opens a pool of 20 connections against a database with max_connections = 300. Why did goodput measure exactly zero for the whole run?
options: The new instances were still warming up and never became useful
Adding instances added retries, and the extra connections pushed the database past the peak of its scalability curve, cutting its throughput from 426 to 247 q/s
The autoscaler hit its max_replicas ceiling and stopped adding capacity
The client timeout was too short for the new instances to respond in time
explanation: Two things compound. First, a retry is not satisfied by being served quickly — it is satisfied by SUCCEEDING — so capacity aimed at retries does not reduce them. Second, database throughput is not linear in connections: past the Universal Scalability Law peak (44 connections here) it goes DOWN, so 400 wanted connections against a 300 limit both got a quarter refused and dragged the surviving 300 to 247 q/s, a 42% cut in the only tier that mattered. Option C is tempting but backwards: hitting the ceiling would have LIMITED the damage. The same trigger with load shedding and the fleet pinned at 6 recovered in 5 seconds at a third of the cost.

question: You must pick ONE change to an autoscaling policy that oscillates badly. Which improves the SLO rather than trading it away?
options: Increase the metric averaging window from 60 s to 300 s to smooth the signal
Compute the desired count from the fleet size that produced the measurement, not the fleet size you have now
Add a 300 s stabilisation window to scale-out as well as scale-in
Lower the CPU target from 60% to 40% so there is more headroom
explanation: The naive formula multiplies current replicas by a ratio measured off the fleet you had a dead time ago, so instances still booting get counted — and ordered — twice. Dividing by the fleet that produced the measurement removes the double-count at zero cost in lag: measured, swing went 395 to 19, launches 1216 to 60, SLO 49.4% to 80.0%. Every other option buys stability by adding lag or money. Option A is the trap: a 300 s average adds ~150 s of dead time, which is the disease, and it cost 13.3 points of SLO in the measurement. Option C delays growth, which is the one direction you must never delay.

question: Your team is deciding how to survive the loss of one availability zone at peak. What does this lesson say about relying on the autoscaler to backfill the lost capacity?
options: It is fine, because autoscalers are designed for exactly this and react in seconds
It is fine as long as max_size is set high enough to cover the lost zone
Do not rely on it: during a zone failure the control plane is saturated, the capacity pool is contended and quotas bind, so min_size must already cover the loss with no scaling action at all
Rely on it, but add a predictive scaling policy so the capacity is forecast in advance
explanation: Autoscaling is a control action taken by a control plane drawing on a shared capacity pool, and it is slowest at exactly the moment you need it fastest — every customer in the region is scaling out at once, and InsufficientInstanceCapacity is a real answer. That is the opposite of static stability. Option B is the most common mistake: max_size is a spend limit and a blast-radius limit, and it does nothing if the API cannot give you instances. Autoscaling is an optimisation for COST; buy reliability with a floor that is already running, warm and in the load balancer before the incident starts.
